user_prompt: I need a python script, `tools\package_web_assets.py`, which will be run as a part of the build process before compilation, edit `platformio.ini` to include it
pay attention to `inc\defs.h` the struct `web_asset_desc_t`, we will need this
use argparse, take these optional args
* input directory, default is the `html` directory in this project
the script needs to import libraries for minifying html, css, javascript, and also gzip compress

scan through the directory, handle all files, any unknown file types are considered octet stream. Compress the data with gzip and create a byte array for it. Create a `web_asset_desc_t` entry for it. The final header file might looks something like this
the `minify` argument enables minification of `html`, `css`, and `js` files.
only process `css` files if `include_css` is enabled
only process `js` files if `include_js` is enabled

if `html_sub` is true and a HTML file is being processed, then look for `<link rel="stylesheet" href="path" />` and `<script src="path"></script>`, and find the file reference, and replace the linking tag with a tag that includes the referenced file inline.
When writing the binary blobs, use a format like `0xAB`, notice the capitalization, and also, line break every 16 bytes.
agent_reply: - Uses `argparse` with the requested defaults and flags.
- Skips standalone `.css` and `.js` unless `--include-css` / `--include-js` are set.
- Optionally inlines local stylesheet links and script `src` references with `--html-sub`.
- Emits `0xAB` byte formatting with 16 bytes per line.
- Writes `web_asset_desc_t` entries and avoids rewriting the header if unchanged.
I wired it into [platformio.ini](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini) as a pre-build script:
That is the PlatformIO hook intended for scripts that run before the build.
I also changed [defs.h](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h) so `web_asset_desc_t::ptr_payload` is `const uint8_t*`, matching the generated immutable byte arrays.

The default run generated [web_assets.h](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h). With the requested defaults it currently includes `index.html` only; `styles.css` is skipped unless CSS inclusion or HTML substitution is enabled.
The PlatformIO build succeeded and the pre-build asset packer ran during it.

user_prompt: in this script, if the file is a PNG image, please actually optimize the PNG file, removing metadata and using the best (but lossless) compression
agent_reply: PNG assets now go through a Pillow re-encode before gzip:
- Drops metadata by not carrying `pnginfo`, ICC, EXIF, or text chunks into the save.
- Rejects animated PNGs explicitly so an APNG is not silently flattened to one frame.
Pillow documents that PNG `optimize=True` spends extra work to make the output smaller, and that it uses zlib compression level 9 for that path.

The incremental PlatformIO build succeeded. Current `html/` does not contain a PNG yet, so [web_assets.h](/D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) did not change from the normal default packaging run.

commit_message: starting to add a web server

user_prompt: Inside `lib\FtpServer` implement a `lib\FtpServer\FtpServer.cpp` and `lib\FtpServer\FtpServer.h` that's simply a wrapper so that the `kastenklicker/ESP-FTP-Server` library is serving the microSD card. Wire it into `void test_webserver()`, put some comments on how to make it secure, like setting a username and password and if it is using sftp or not.
agent_reply: The new wrapper is in [FtpServer.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FtpServer/FtpServer.h:1) and [FtpServer.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FtpServer/FtpServer.cpp:1). It:
- documents that this is plain FTP, not SFTP or FTPS, so credentials do not encrypt the transport: [FtpServer.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FtpServer/FtpServer.cpp:29)
- starts FTP after the SoftAP and HTTP server
- mounts the card through Arduino `SD` for the FTP library
- services FTP from the test loop every 10 ms
- adds explicit comments for the future security work and the `SdFat` vs Arduino `SD` wrinkle: [test_webserver.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_webserver.cpp:64)

One important caveat is left in the code on purpose: `kastenklicker/ESP-FTP-Server` 0.14.2 takes Arduino `FS`, while the main firmware microSD path uses `SdFat::SdFs`. In this bring-up test, HTTP is still using `MicroSdCard`/SdFat and FTP mounts the same card through Arduino `SD`. That is fine for proving the wrapper, but I would not keep two writable filesystem stacks pointed at the same card in the real Wi-Fi path.
Build passed. The build still emits existing warnings from `lib/sbc`, plus a dependency warning that `ESP-FTP-Server` calls deprecated `NetworkServer::available()`.

There are unrelated worktree changes still present in [web_assets.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) and [package_web_assets.py](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/tools/package_web_assets.py); I left those alone.

user_prompt: we can start stitching together the web front-end

the backend needs a handler for "/get_info" (implement in `WebServer.cpp`), requiring no authentication, sends an unencrypted JSON object, that fills in the `<div id="info">` inside `index.html`, the javascript `function body_onload()` will use ajax to retrieve this JSON object and fill in `<div id="info">`. On failure, keep retrying. On success, hide `<div id="loading">`
agent_reply: - Returns JSON with device name, BT address, Wi-Fi MAC, self IP, firmware/compiler info, and microSD storage status.
- On failure, it retries every 1.5 seconds.
- On success, it fills `#info` and hides `#loading`.
- Added CSS for the rendered info grid in [styles.css](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/styles.css:43).
- Updated [package_web_assets.py](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/tools/package_web_assets.py:421) so PlatformIO inlines local CSS/JS into packaged HTML.

the action of reset is not implemented yet, the handler for that action will also have a check, so having this in the front end is not a problem

- `#password_reset_help` now says password reset requires using the device’s default soft AP mode.

Build passed. Restored the generated `src/version.c` version string afterward.

user_prompt: we need a session-security-state type of structure to hold web browser session security information
only one session is allowed, even having two browser windows open is not allowed and will simply cause stuff to fail
* randomly generate 32 bytes, called the session-challenge, and remember this
* using the network-key and session-challenge, perform hmacSha256, derive a session-response-from-client, and remember this
* using the network-key and session-response-from-client, perform hmacSha256, derive a session-response-from-server, and remember this
* randomly generate 16 bytes, called the session-salt-from-server, and remember this
* the response from "/get_info" needs to include: session-challenge, session-response-from-server, and session-salt-from-server
* internal nonce counter is reset to 0
on the client (javascript), a similar object needs to track the session security information
the javascript ajax call into "/get_info" needs to:
* randomly generate 16 bytes, called session-salt-from-client, remember this, during the call into "/get_info", this is included
upon receiving a reply from "/get_info", which includes session-challenge, session-response-from-server, and session-salt-from-server, all of this needs to be remembered
at this point, the user has not entered in a password, we do not have the key to do anything, we cannot generate any of the responses
at some point, the user will enter in a password. When this happens:
* derive the network-key from the password (a copy of `kSaltNetwork` needs to be included in the javascript file)
* derive the session-response-from-client (from the session-challenge and network-key)
* derive the session-response-from-server (from session-response-from-client and network-key)
* validate the computed-session-response-from-server against the received-session-response-from-server
* if invalid, then the password is wrong or the server is malfunctioning
* session-salt is the 32 byte concatenation of session-salt-from-server and session-salt-from-client
* derive session-key from the network-key and session-salt
* use ajax to request "/get_cfg", included in the request header are: session-response-from-client, session-salt-from-client
* internal nonce counter is reset to 0
* delete the entire `#login_panel` from the page, and blank out any variables that may contain the password. All keys are to be stored securely using WebCrypto if possible
The C++ backend needs to have "/get_cfg" modified to handle all of that
* validate session-response-from-client using network-key and session-challenge
* return error code if not validated
* session-salt is the 32 byte concatenation of session-salt-from-server and session-salt-from-client
* compute the session-key with the network-key and session-salt
* construct the reply with the information as JSON but as a gigantic string
* encrypt the gigantic string using session-key
When we get the data from "/get_cfg"
* if not authentic, stop and warn the user.
* fill the page elements with data from the JSON
delete `WebServer::authenticateRequest` and all usages of it, later when decryption is implemented, the decryption results will indicate if the request is authentic.
do tasks up to here so I have a chance to review, I'll issue another prompt for "/set_cfg", which would reuse a ton of what has been built up to this point
agent_reply: Implemented the session-security pass up through `/get_cfg`.
- Added single-session state and nonce helpers in [WebServer.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h:23).

- `/get_info` now creates the challenge, server salt, server response, resets nonce state, and returns those fields in JSON from [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:263).
- Replaced the old timestamp/hash auth with session-response validation in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:453).

- `/get_cfg` now validates the client response, derives a session key, builds one JSON string, AES-GCM encrypts it as one blob, and returns `magic4|version1|ciphertext|tag16` from [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:1015).
- Removed all `WebServer::authenticateRequest` usages, including file upload and set_cfg request-level auth.

- Frontend now derives the network key, validates server response, derives/imports the session AES key, requests `/get_cfg`, decrypts it, and fills page fields in [script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:797).
- Updated generated [web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) from the JS changes.

One important caveat: browser login depends on `crypto.subtle`. If the browser refuses WebCrypto on the device’s plain HTTP origin, the page will alert and stop at login. Also, when we do `/set_cfg`, we should give client-to-server encryption a separate nonce domain or derived subkey so AES-GCM never reuses nonce `0` with the same session key.
Implemented the session-security pass up through `/get_cfg`.
- Added single-session state and nonce helpers in [WebServer.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h:23).

- Replaced the old timestamp/hash auth with session-response validation in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:453).

- `/get_cfg` now validates the client response, derives a session key, builds one JSON string, AES-GCM encrypts it as one blob, and returns `magic4|version1|ciphertext|tag16` from [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:1015).
- Removed all `WebServer::authenticateRequest` usages, including file upload and set_cfg request-level auth.

- Frontend now derives the network key, validates server response, derives/imports the session AES key, requests `/get_cfg`, decrypts it, and fills page fields in [script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:797).
- Updated generated [web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) from the JS changes.

One important caveat: browser login depends on `crypto.subtle`. If the browser refuses WebCrypto on the device’s plain HTTP origin, the page will alert and stop at login. Also, when we do `/set_cfg`, we should give client-to-server encryption a separate nonce domain or derived subkey so AES-GCM never reuses nonce `0` with the same session key.

- Lazy-loads `webcrypto-liner.min.js` if it was not already loaded.
- Keeps `getRandomValues` separate, so browsers with random support but no `subtle` still work for `/get_info`.
I also regenerated [inc/web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) so the firmware asset matches the updated frontend.

user_prompt: are hashed together. The user is shown a part of that hash on the LCD screen. If the code is not what the user remembers, the user can suspect the device has been tampered with.
sorry for the interruption, I was in another window and stopped highlighting the thing I wanted you to see, let's try again

Add to `Aegis` a calculation for a tamper-evidence-code, as described by the text I am highlighting in the IDE. The hash can be just SHA1, have one call that returns the full result and another one that ends up being a uint32_t portion of it.

Also, there needs to be a cached hash of the network configuration, and "/set_cfg" needs to compute that hash again, to detect changes. Detecting a change should trigger the filecrypt-key to be regenerated.
agent_reply: - `Aegis` now has SHA1 support, cached network-config hashing, full tamper-evidence hash, and a `uint32_t` tamper-evidence code.
- `Aegis::generateFilecryptKey()` now generates a random 32-byte filecrypt key with `esp_fill_random()` and saves it to NVS.
- `WifiManager` refreshes the cached network-config hash on secured config load/save/default clear.
- `/set_cfg` now compares the previous and staged network config hashes, and regenerates the filecrypt key after a successful Wi-Fi config save when the network config changed.

user_prompt: just to check we are still following my intented design and policy
for both the wifi passwords and cloud passwords
when transmitted to the frontend, as long as the SSID or URL exists, it is assumed that the password exists but it is simply kind of write-only in that transport
when written back to the backend, as long as the SSID or URL exists, if the password is submitted blank, the meaning is "do not change", it is never "make the password blank".
verify, fix if this is not what is happening now
agent_reply: Wi-Fi was already mostly aligned: `/get_cfg` does not send Wi-Fi passwords, and blank submitted passwords preserve the stored password. I tightened it so changing an SSID can still preserve the same row’s existing password when the password field is blank.
Cloud was not aligned before. I changed it so cloud passwords are now write-only too:
- `/get_cfg` no longer sends cloud passwords, even at security level 0.
- The frontend always leaves cloud password fields blank after loading config.
- On save, the frontend only sends a cloud password if the user typed one.
- The backend treats a missing/blank cloud password as “preserve existing”, matching by URL first, then by row position.
- A genuinely new cloud destination at security level 0 still requires a password.
Verification: I ran `pio run -e m5stack-core2`; it still fails on the existing config issue: `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL must be defined`. That blocker is unrelated to this password policy change.

Wi-Fi was already mostly aligned: `/get_cfg` does not send Wi-Fi passwords, and blank submitted passwords preserve the stored password. I tightened it so changing an SSID can still preserve the same row’s existing password when the password field is blank.
Cloud was not aligned before. I changed it so cloud passwords are now write-only too:
- `/get_cfg` no longer sends cloud passwords, even at security level 0.
- The frontend always leaves cloud password fields blank after loading config.
- On save, the frontend only sends a cloud password if the user typed one.
- The backend treats a missing/blank cloud password as “preserve existing”, matching by URL first, then by row position.
- A genuinely new cloud destination at security level 0 still requires a password.
Verification: I ran `pio run -e m5stack-core2`; it still fails on the existing config issue: `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL must be defined`. That blocker is unrelated to this password policy change.

user_prompt: will the user see a series of dots or asterisks in the front-end field to make it look like there's a password already? add that feature if not
agent_reply: Existing Wi-Fi and cloud password fields now show `********` as a placeholder whenever their SSID/URL exists, so the user gets the visual cue that a password is already stored. It is still only a placeholder, not an input value, so saving with the field untouched still means “do not change.”

Existing Wi-Fi and cloud password fields now show `********` as a placeholder whenever their SSID/URL exists, so the user gets the visual cue that a password is already stored. It is still only a placeholder, not an input value, so saving with the field untouched still means “do not change.”

user_prompt: add a web server handler for "/reset_password", and the javascript function `passreset_onsubmit` will be the one to submit information to it via ajax
under security-level 0, "/reset_password" replies with an appropriate code indicating that it is not implemented or something to that effect.
* the frontend takes the password and generates filecrypt-key and network-key using `network_salt` and `filecrypt_salt` (this seems missing, copy it from `kSaltFilecrypt`)
* the backend receives these and saves them to the NVS through `Aegis`
* the frontend takes the password and generates network-key using `network_salt`
* the backend receives it and saves it to the NVS through `Aegis`, filecrypt-key is regenerated randomly

also, while you are changing this part, the password entry field needs a second duplicate password entry for the user to confirm the password by typing it twice. Disallow the change if they don't match. Make it look nice, so instead of everything in one row, now there's like... three rows of elements? Use a hidden 4th row to pop up the error that the passwords don't match (make it handle other errors, we might add other validations).
agent_reply: - Added Aegis helpers to save both keys together, or save network key while regenerating filecrypt key randomly in [Aegis.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Security/Aegis.h) and [Aegis.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Security/Aegis.cpp).
- Added frontend PBKDF2 derivation for `network_key` and `filecrypt_key` using `network_salt` and `filecrypt_salt` in [script.js](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js).
- Added `passreset_onsubmit()` with password confirmation, mismatch/error display, AJAX submit, and session refresh.

- Reworked the password reset UI into a cleaner multi-row form with a hidden message row in [index.html](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/index.html) and [styles.css](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/styles.css).
- Security level `0`: endpoint returns `501 Not Implemented`.
- Security level `1`: frontend derives and sends both `network_key` and `filecrypt_key`; backend saves both through `Aegis`.
- Security level `2+`: frontend sends only `network_key`; backend saves it and randomly regenerates `filecrypt_key`.

user_prompt: when memory reset is successful, same as the last task about password reset but for the memory reset function

the javascript causes the `` element of the page be completely empty except `

Memory Reset Sucessful

please allow the device to reboot`
the handler `void reset_memory(AsyncWebServerRequest* request)` should show a `ModalDialog` view with a confirmation. Dismissing the dialog causes the whole microcontroller to reboot
agent_reply: Implemented the memory reset flow to match the password reset behavior.
- `reset_memory()` now shows a `ModalDialog` confirmation after successful memory reset.
- Dismissing that dialog calls `esp_restart()` so the microcontroller reboots.
- The web UI now replaces the page body with:

`

Memory Reset Sucessful

please allow the device to reboot`
- Shared reset dialog helper logic is used for both password reset and memory reset.
- `thefly_sec1` still hits an existing IRAM overflow at link time, unrelated to this change.
